title: A Proof of the RM Code Capacity Conjecture
speaker: Emmanuel Abbe
abstract: In 1948, Shannon used a probabilistic argument to show the existence of codes achieving channel capacity. In 1954, Muller and Reed introduced a simple deterministic code construction, conjectured shortly after to achieve channel capacity. Major progress was made towards establishing this conjecture over the last decades, with the involvement of various areas of discrete mathematics. In particular, the special case of the erasure channel was settled in 2015 by Kudekar et al., relying on Bourgain-Kalai's sharp threshold theorem for symmetric monotone properties.

proof_stages.label: Shannon capacity benchmark and explicit-code problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates worst-case and average-case binary channel coding on the Boolean hypercube, derives the random-code entropy rate 1-H(epsilon), and reframes the goal as finding an explicit linear construction attaining this benchmark.

proof_stages.label: Reed-Muller codes and the capacity conjecture
proof_stages.summary: Hadamard/simplex intuition leads to Reed-Muller codes as evaluations of bounded-degree Boolean polynomials, and the central conjecture is that sequences of these explicit codes achieve Shannon capacity for the binary error channel and, more generally, symmetric channels.

proof_stages.label: Weight-enumerator route and its barrier
proof_stages.summary: Linearity lets the global decoding error be bounded by grouping competing codewords according to Hamming weight, but known Reed-Muller weight-enumerator and hypercontractive bounds approached yet stopped short of the Shannon capacity curve.

proof_stages.label: Local-error reformulation and sharp-threshold evidence
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces local coordinate decoding, explains how sufficiently fast local-error decay would imply global decoding success, and reviews sharp-threshold proofs for erasures and the obstruction that bit-flip errors are intrinsically nonmonotone.

proof_stages.label: Recursive polarization viewpoint and obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The polar-code tensor recursion expresses conditional entropies through a polarization process, motivating an analogous heavy-row ordering for Reed-Muller codes, but proving that the good polarized rows coincide with Reed-Muller rows runs into hard additive-combinatorial estimates.

proof_stages.label: Weak local-threshold base case
proof_stages.summary: A symmetry-and-entropy argument shows that any symmetric code used below channel capacity has local coordinate error strictly less than 1/2, providing the weak initial advantage needed for boosting.

proof_stages.label: Sunflower boosting of local decoding
proof_stages.summary: Reed-Muller self-similarity under restriction to subspaces supplies many smaller local decoders, and arranging their supports as sunflower petals isolates overlaps in a common kernel so that, after conditioning, their weak biases can be amplified by voting.

proof_stages.label: Fourier concentration, recursive amplification, and final assembly
proof_stages.summary: Fourier/Chebyshev estimates and affine symmetry show that typical kernels preserve the weak bias; iterated higher-moment sunflower boosting yields strong local error decay, which is combined with refined weight-enumerator bounds to obtain exponential global error decay and hence Reed-Muller capacity for symmetric channels.

examples.name: Erasure channel as the monotone case
examples.note: For erasures, the local decoding error is a monotone Boolean property of the erasure pattern, which visibly generates the sharp-threshold approach and contrasts with the nonmonotone bit-flip channel.

examples.name: Independent weak decoders and majority vote
examples.note: The elementary fact that many independent voters each beating probability 1/2 give exponentially small majority error motivates the boosting mechanism that sunflowers are designed to approximate.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the weak-decoders majority-vote scenario to explain boosting and then traces how independent petals after conditioning yield error improvement
